title: Realigning AI technology towards the Sustainable Development Goals - Nature Machine Intelligence
gist: A commentary co-authored by Yoshua Bengio and colleagues argues that AI's rapid infrastructure expansion is undermining several UN Sustainable Development Goals via energy/e-waste growth, synthetic-media-driven erosion of public trust, and the opacity of black-box models, and calls for realigning AI development with the SDGs.

insights.claim: Data centres accounted for approximately 1.5% of global electricity consumption in 2024, and their energy demand is projected to more than double by 2030.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Confirms AI-driven power demand growth is a multi-year structural tailwind for grid capacity, power generation and cooling infrastructure investment, and a headwind for decarbonization timelines.
insights.quote: Data centres accounted for approximately 1.5% of global electricity consumption in 2024, with their energy demand projected to more than double by 2030.

insights.claim: Under aggressive growth scenarios, electronic waste from generative AI could increase by nearly three orders of magnitude between 2020 and 2030, reaching an estimated five million tonnes, while less than a quarter of e-waste is currently formally collected and recycled.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: A near-1000x e-waste growth scenario signals a large addressable gap for electronics recycling, hardware recovery and circular-economy services tied to AI compute buildout.
insights.quote: Under aggressive growth scenarios, e-waste from generative AI could increase by nearly three orders of magnitude between 2020 and 2030, reaching an estimated five million tonnes5.

insights.claim: Informal e-waste practices such as open burning, landfilling and uncontrolled dumping are already releasing hazardous substances into soil, water and air because formal recycling capacity has not kept pace with AI hardware turnover.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Regulatory and reputational risk is building around AI hardware suppliers and cloud operators that lack formal end-of-life recycling programs.

themes.theme: Synthetic media and erosion of trust

insights.claim: AI can now generate highly realistic but factually incorrect text, images and video at low cost, and the resulting synthetic content is difficult to regulate, with the central risk being a collapse of public trust in authentic information.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Frames trust-and-provenance infrastructure (watermarking, content authentication, detection tooling) as a structural need rather than a niche feature as generative content costs keep falling.
insights.quote: AI technology can be used to effortlessly generate highly realistic but factually incorrect texts, images and videos at low cost, which blurs the boundary between truth and fabrication.

insights.claim: The opacity and limited explainability of black-box AI algorithms makes it nearly impossible to trace reasoning or assign accountability when biased outputs or false claims arise, which the authors say fundamentally threatens institutional accountability and governance frameworks.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Reinforces the investment case for interpretability/explainability tooling and audit infrastructure as a prerequisite for AI deployment in accountable institutional settings (finance, government, healthcare).
insights.quote: When biased outputs or false claims arise, inscrutable decision pathways make it nearly impossible to trace reasoning or assign accountability.

insights.claim: The authors, including Yoshua Bengio, call for realigning AI technology development with the UN Sustainable Development Goals, treating energy/e-waste, misinformation and opacity as a linked set of risks requiring coordinated solutions rather than isolated fixes.
insights.speaker: Nature Machine Intelligence
insights.so_what: Signals growing academic/policy pressure for AI governance frameworks that bundle environmental, information-integrity and accountability requirements together, which could shape future AI regulation.
